posts.hook: Same project. Third version. Finally makes sense.
posts.body:
Same project. Third version. Finally makes sense.

V1: one massive prompt, everything fed in. $1 per report. Architecture: embarrassing.

V2: discovered subworkflows. Split prompts. Static data separated from dynamic. Cost dropped.

V3: verification before fetching. The model only sees what it needs.

$0.06 per report now.

I spent weeks before this following tutorials. Built every template. Understood nothing — because their data flows weren't breaking. Mine weren't either, because they weren't mine.

Nobody ships a good automation on the first try. Most tutorials show the finished thing. They skip the three versions before it.

The frustration is the curriculum.

Data mapping errors. Variable names pointing nowhere. A node running forever on malformed input. That's the actual class.

V4 is coming.

What version are you on?

#n8n #buildinpublic
posts.topic: n8n stock analysis automation — 3-version evolution, $1 → $0.06 cost reduction

posts.hook: Day 1 of building Setu in public.
posts.body:
Day 1 of building Setu in public.

Here's what I shipped:

A content system that routes every AI model call through a fallback chain. Primary fails → secondary. Secondary fails → tertiary. Automatic.

8 task types. 3 models each. One router.

Also wrote 11 workflow SOPs. Platform rules, content pillars, audit schedules. The kind of documentation that makes the second week faster than the first.

$5/day circuit breaker on Claude API. Costs stay tracked. No surprise bills.

That's how I build — foundation before facade.

Do you build the infrastructure first, or ship and fix?

#buildinpublic #setu
posts.topic: Day 1 Setu launch — infrastructure shipped: model router, 11 SOPs, cost circuit breaker

posts.body: Brand values post — source: setu-brand-values-current-work.png. Values: Work not tech, Quiet over loud, Respect owners, Ship don't slide, Map before build. Current work: Bridge Zero + Setu Library.
posts.topic: Setu brand values — quiet principles for practical AI

posts.body:
Same workflow. Three versions. 94% cheaper.

I recorded the walkthrough for my n8n stock analysis workflow.

Version 1 was one giant prompt.
Static company data, live prices, analysis rules, formatting rules. Everything went into one envelope.

It worked as a demo.
It cost about $1 per report.

Version 2 split the work into subworkflows: financials, stock data, index data, and formatting.

Better structure. Still not enough.

Version 3 added one small question before anything ran:

Do I actually need to generate this report again?

That changed the cost from $1 to $0.06 per report.
Same baseline model. Same output. 94% lower cost.

The useful part was not the final workflow.

It was the two bad versions before it.

Variable names pointing nowhere.
Data mapping errors.
Nodes running too long on malformed input.
Three nights lost to one character in one field name.

Most tutorials show the finished automation.
They skip the versions that taught you why it works.

That is the part I am trying to document.

V4 is already being rebuilt on a different architecture.

What version are you on?

#n8n #buildinpublic #AIautomation
posts.topic: Loom walkthrough: n8n stock analysis workflow evolution from $1 to $0.06 per report

asset.note: Final published image selected by user; exact published asset not stored in repo.

posts.body:
Setu means bridge.

Most architecture and interior firms I know aren't behind on AI. They're buried in work a computer should be doing.

You build the mood board by hand. You copy the material schedule into the BOQ. Then into the quote. Then into the email. You chase three vendors for prices. On Friday you turn a folder of site photos into a client update nobody enjoys writing.

The work is real. The tooling around it isn't.

So I'm building one. An AI system that takes a project from mood board to palette to a material, lighting and BOQ sheet to FF&E to a render that actually matches your floor plan.

I'll be honest about where it stands. The first three pieces work. The render is still in progress. Early versions kept sliding furniture off the plan, and it took a few attempts to make the AI respect the layout instead of redrawing the room.

No client yet. No launch. Just building it in the open, one workflow at a time.

If any of this sounds like your week, I'd like to hear how you work.

#interiordesign #architecture #buildinpublic #aiforbusiness
posts.topic: Bridge-1 intro — building AI system for interior design/architecture firms, honest build status, ICP pain named

posts.hook: Here is the brief for a real 3BHK.
posts.body:
Here is the brief for a real 3BHK.

A list of forwarded WhatsApp messages:
- "Loft in utility."
- "Pooja room as per vastu."
- "Study in the children’s bedroom."
- "Vanity near the wash basin."
- "Shoe rack."

No form. No structured doc. Half the details were never written down. The rest is left to the designer to imagine.

I ran it through the system I’m building.

It fills the gaps the way a good designer would—handling vastu (traditional spatial geometry based on solar alignment and wind patterns), circulation, standard layouts—and turns the wants into structured schedules in minutes.

Here are the real specifics of this run:
- 3BHK · East-facing · 2100 sq ft · ₹15L interior budget.
- Material & Lighting Schedules published directly to Google Sheets.
- A BOQ Draft with real quantities.
- FF&E sourcing sheet and auto-written elevation render prompts.

Honest line: The BOQ is left blank in some fields on purpose. A physical site measure is always required before ordering. And we didn’t generate the final render today—the AI kept sliding the furniture off the plan (we’ll show how we’re working through it tomorrow).

It is a first draft, not a final. They revise from there.

Not replacing anyone. Just getting to the first draft in an afternoon, not a fortnight.

Building in the open. Run a design or architecture practice? Message me—I want to test it on real work.

#interiordesign #architecture #automation #buildinpublic
posts.topic: Real 3BHK WhatsApp brief → Google Sheets Material Schedule, Lighting Schedule, BOQ Draft, FF&E

posts.hook: Everyone keeps asking to see the renders.
posts.body:
Everyone keeps asking to see the renders.

I'm not showing them yet. Here's the honest reason.

The floor plan for this living room is specific:

- An L-sofa on the right, facing the TV
- Behind the sofa is a half-wall, not a full one — half wall, half opening straight into the dining. The opening is the point.
- The TV console on the entry wall, just east of the main door
- The pooja unit in the far-left corner, by the entrance
- A center table tucked inside the L

Easy for a human to read. I handed it to the model as a clean control image and asked for V1.

V1 came back:

- The sofa flipped to the wrong side of the room
- The pooja unit vanished completely
- The model invented doors in the back wall that don't exist
- And the open dining — the entire point of this layout — got sealed into a solid wall

It wasn't a styling problem. The room looked good. It was a spatial lie.

The model never treated the plan as a contract. It treated it as a mood.

That's the moment I made the call: no renders go out until the geometry holds. A beautiful render with the sofa on the wrong wall isn't a demo — it's a trap. The client approves something that can't be built, and the trust is gone the day they walk into the real room.

So that's where we are. No hero shot. No carousel of dream interiors.

Just V1, and the exact list of what it broke.

The render isn't the milestone. A render you can trust is the milestone.

Update: V2 changed the approach. I stopped asking the model to style the plan and started giving it the coordinates — then checking them. This time the geometry held.

Next post: the exact spatial method that made the plan stick.

That's the build. In public. Including the part where it doesn't work — and the part where it starts to.

#interiordesign #architecture #buildinpublic #aiautomation
posts.topic: Honest build-in-public: why no AI renders ship yet — V1 spatial failures (sofa flipped, pooja vanished, invented doors, dining sealed). Spatial claims verified vs plan-coords.json.
